fertilizer: Fertilization should follow growth stages, shifting from nitrogen-heavy in the vegetative phase (e.g., 3-1-2 or 4-1-3 N-P-K) to potassium-heavy during flowering and fruiting (e.g., 1-1-2, 1-1-3, or 1-1-4 N-P-K). MasterBlend 4-18-38 is commonly used in hydroponics. For soil, NPK rates depend on system and soil testing, often split into applications or provided via fertigation.
watering: Watering should be managed closely, supplying only enough to satisfy crop requirements, as excess irrigation can leach nutrients. Drip irrigation (fertigation) is recommended for efficiency in plasticulture and substrate systems. Optimal water-fertilizer combinations vary by environment, with some studies suggesting total seasonal water use in specific greenhouse settings around 12-13 L per plant.
pests: Twospotted spider mite, Lygus bug, Strawberry bud weevil (clipper), Aphids, White grubs, Wireworms, Root weevils, Thrips, Whiteflies, Cyclamen mite, Cutworms, Armyworms, Corn earworm, Strawberry rootworm, Slugs, Snails
diseases: Anthracnose (fruit rot and crown rot), Botrytis fruit rot (gray mold), Red stele (Phytophthora root rot), Black root rot, Powdery mildew, Angular leaf spot, Leaf spot (Mycosphaerella), Verticillium wilt, Charcoal rot, Phomopsis leaf blight, Pestalotia leaf spot and fruit rot
growth_stages: Strawberry plants follow a lifecycle where the crown produces leaves, flowers, fruit, and runners (stolons). Runners root to form daughter plants. The plant typically requires an establishment period; for example, blossoms are often removed in the first year to encourage vegetative growth and runner formation, with fruit production beginning in subsequent years for June-bearers or within the first year for day-neutral and some everbearing types. Plants may eventually become crowded and less productive, requiring replacement.
planting: Plant in early spring as soon as the soil can be prepared. For the matted-row system, set plants 18 to 24 inches apart within rows spaced 3 to 4 feet apart. For the hill system, space plants 12 to 15 inches apart, or 25 cm apart in specific configurations, while removing all runners. Day-neutral cultivars are often spaced 5 to 9 inches apart. Plants should be set with the midpoint of the crown level with the soil surface.
harvest: Harvest timing depends on the type: June-bearing strawberries produce one crop in late spring/early summer; everbearers produce in early summer and late summer/fall; and day-neutrals can produce throughout the growing season if conditions allow. Generally, fruits begin ripening about 5 weeks after bloom, and the harvest period typically lasts about 3 weeks. Berries should be picked 1 to 2 days after they turn fully red.
propagation: Strawberries are primarily propagated vegetatively using runners (stolons), which develop daughter plants when nodes touch the soil. While seeds can be used, they require stratification and have a longer lead time. Professional nurseries also utilize tissue culture. It is recommended to purchase certified, disease-free plants from reputable nurseries rather than transplanting from old beds.
varieties: June-bearing: Annapolis, Allstar, Earliglow, Jewel, Honeoye, AC Wendy, Flavorfest, Surecrop, Cavendish, Crimson King
Day-neutral/Everbearing: Seascape, Tribute, Tristar, Albion, Evie 2, Ogallala
Florida-specific: Florida Brilliance, Florida Ember, Florida Encore, Florida Medallion, Sweet Sensation Florida127
